summary: Oracle's sudden mass layoff of approximately 90% of Solaris engineering marks the effective death of the proprietary operating system, with employees learning of their termination via pre-recorded calls in what Cantrill calls a 'depraved act of corporate violence.' The post contrasts the fragility of proprietary software—vulnerable to corporate whims and capable of dying instantly—with the resilience of open source, which 'cannot, even, die.' Cantrill argues that Solaris achieved immortality through its open source period, with illumos and technologies like ZFS and DTrace continuing to thrive despite Oracle's attempts to kill the community.
key_points: Oracle laid off ~90% of Solaris engineering, effectively killing the OS
Employees were notified via pre-recorded calls ('robo-RIF'd')
Proprietary software is inherently fragile and vulnerable to corporate decisions
Open source software is immortal—it can be neglected but never truly dies
illumos community survived Oracle's re-proprietarization and continues to thrive
Solaris's true life was its open source period, and its technologies live on
money_quotes: It is easier to destroy than to create.
In the long run – to paraphrase Keynes – proprietary software projects are all dead.
In stark contrast to proprietary software, open source does not – cannot, even – die.
Open source communities are robust because they are often united not only by circumstance, but by values.
Solaris may not have been truly born until it was made open source, and – certainly to me, anyway – it died the moment it was again made proprietary.
Born out of wedlock between Sun and AT&T and dying at the hands of a remorseless corporate sociopath a quarter century later.
key_insight: While proprietary software can be killed instantly by corporate fiat, open source software achieves a form of immortality—surviving through community values and living on as both working code and technical literature for future generations.
